articles.title: SQLite should have (Rust-style) editions
articles.summary: SQLite's default foreign key constraints are often ignored by default, which can lead to dangling references and other issues in applications that rely on consistency. This is particularly problematic for projects where data integrity is crucial, such as embedded systems or financial software. The lack of enforced foreign key constraints also means that developers must manually manage user IDs and delete them when necessary, which can be error-prone and time-consuming.

articles.summary: Inkling's new open-weights model, called Inkling, is a Mixture-of-Experts transformer with 975B total parameters and 41B active. It supports a context window of up to 1M tokens and was pretrained on 45 trillion tokens of text, images, audio, and video. articles.title: Show HN: misa77 - a codec that decodes 2x faster than LZ4 (at better ratios)
articles.summary: Misa77 is an LZ-based codec that targets the write-once, read-many niche with extremely high decompression throughput and modest compression ratios. It offers particularly high decompression throughput on highly compressible files and has a somewhat synergizing tendency to decompress highly compressed files faster than other codecs like LZ4. This makes it attractive for high-effort compression and experimental compression modes that aim to spend more effort at compression time.

articles.title: Microsoft Confirms Windows GDID Device Identifier That Cannot Be Disabled, Documented in FBI Case Filing
articles.summary: Microsoft has confirmed that it assigns a unique device-specific ID to each Windows installation using its Global Device Identifier (GDID), which cannot be disabled without affecting Windows activation and Microsoft Store apps. The GDID is generated by a chain of services, including wlidsvc, login.live.com, and the Connected Devices Platform, and is stored in the Windows registry under HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\IdentityCRL\ExtendedProperties. This identifier has been used by law enforcement to track alleged Scattered Spider hacking group member Peter Stokes across various networks and locations over a period of eight months.

articles.title: Towards a harness that can do anything
articles.summary: A good harness must naturally integrate with the LLM's agent, be transparent in how it operates, and be as lean as possible while maintaining flexibility. It should also prioritize error survival, update survival, and prevent memory corruption or degradation over time. Preliminary truths include determinism, where the LLM chooses its goals and selects skills at runtime, and the importance of a priori coding knowledge. A good harness must make use of this knowledge to wrangle the LLM effectively, while also delegating tasks efficiently and making it easy for the LLM to do its job.

articles.title: Comparing Obelisk with Temporal and Restate
articles.summary: Comparing Obelisk with Temporal and Restate all let a function keep making progress after its process disappears. The largest architectural difference is that Obelisk is a workflow runtime: it loads and runs application components itself. articles.title: a bunch of stuff i used to not know about K&R C
articles.summary: C's grammar is context-sensitive and ambiguous due to its single-pass compiler design, which requires tracking scopes and identifiers while parsing. This was a necessity for heavily memory-constrained systems like those in the original C compiler. The fact that identifiers can refer to typedefs doesn't matter since it's not much extra work to check; however, modern C's scoping rules are particularly problematic due to its discontinuous scope rules. Void is an ANSI invention, likely introduced as a way to simplify function declarations without requiring explicit return types.

articles.title: i've been thinking about null pointers
articles.summary: Null pointers are a fundamental concept in programming languages, but their weirdness extends beyond safety concerns. The issue of null is often used to indicate errors or lack of existence, but it also communicates semantic information that depends on context. This leads to the other strange aspect of null: pointer types can have any value except for one, which is specified as its own thing with different semantics than other pointers.
